comments.content:
[IMAGE:0]Midnight is just a few moments away, and so it's time to launch a new Magic Monday. Ask me anything about occultism, and with certain exceptions, any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer. Please note:  Any question or comment received after then will not get an answer, and in fact will just be deleted.  If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 143,916th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.1 of The Magic Monday FAQ here. 

Also: I will not be putting through or answering any more questions about practicing magic around children. I've answered those in simple declarative sentences in the FAQ. If you read the FAQ and don't think your question has been answered, read it again. If that doesn't help, consider remedial reading classes; yes, it really is as simple and straightforward as the FAQ says.  And further:  I've decided that questions involving the evocation of spirits are permanently off topic here. The point of occultism is to develop your own capacities, not to try to bully or wheedle other beings into doing things for you. I've discussed this in a post on my blog.

The image? I field a lot of questions about my books these days, so I've decided to do little capsule summaries of them here, one per week. The book above on the left was my thirty-third published book and my second published novel. It had a long genesis:  I wrote it as a series of one-a-month blog posts over a four year period, from late 2004 to early 2009. I'd previously done several brief fictional pieces on The Archdruid Report, the blog I had in those days, using the tools of fiction to talk about the future we were heading for; I decided to see if I could do something a little longer and more ambitious -- and Star's Reach was the result. It's set in the 2470s in Meriga, which is what they call "America" by then; two civil wars and a long process of technological decline have landed most Merigans in something not that different from a medieval society, though it's by no means identical to medieval Europe. In that future, a very large share of raw materials come from urban ruins, extracted by members of the guild of ruinmen...and it's while nearly getting killed dismantling a ruin that one young ruinman, Trey sunna Gwen, finds a clue to a legendary place where humanity might once have talked with intelligent beings from another world -- a place called Star's Reach.

It's an early novel of mine and there are aspects of it that seem clumsy to me now, but on the whole it still works very well. It's held together largely by Trey's distinctive first-person voice as he narrates his journey and his reflections, by turns clueless and shrewd, on the world of his time and the old world it replaced. Just at the moment it's temporarily out of print, but will be back on the shelves in November of this year; you can preorder a copy here if you live in the US and here if you live elsewhere.

Buy Me A Coffee

Ko-Fi

I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use either of the links above to access my online tip jar; Buymeacoffee is good for small tips, Ko-Fi is better for larger ones. (I used to use PayPal but they developed an allergy to free speech, so I've developed an allergy to them.) If you're interested in political and economic astrology, or simply prefer to use a subscription service to support your favorite authors, you can find my Patreon page here and my SubscribeStar page here. 
 
[IMAGE:1]I've also had quite a few people over the years ask me where they should buy my books, and here's the answer. Bookshop.org is an alternative online bookstore that supports local bookstores and authors, which a certain gargantuan corporation doesn't, and I have a shop there, which you can check out here. Please consider patronizing it if you'd like to purchase any of my books online.

And don't forget to look up your Pangalactic New Age Soul Signature at CosmicOom.com.

With that said, have at it!

***This Magic Monday is now closed--as in, no further comments will be put through. See you next week!***

I am currently reading some Behaviorists, because their theory played a massive role in shaping certain aspects of our society I'm currently writing about. They seem to genuinely believe that human beings have no inner life; and this ideology, which seems so alien, not jut to me in terms of culture but in terms of basic experience; but it was profoundly influential in the 20th century. I'm currently reading B. F. Skinner, and it's profoundly weird to read him, because he seems to think in a way that seems inhuman. Some of the work he wrote includes things like extensive discussions on how ideas and thinking simply does not exist, without once stopping to acknowledge that what he was doing seems to imply the existence of thoughts, because in order to write he needs to think.

How could people possibly have become so twisted up that they fail to notice that in order to write about why people do not think, they have to be thinking themselves? It's profoundly creepy, and for the life of me I cannot make sense of how it could have possibly made sense to so many people.

replies.content: I'm coming to think of this in terms of Carl Jung's theory of projection. Many people project their own mental contents onto the sensory world, and then insist that those projections are in fact qualities of the outside world and not things that they have projected onto the world from within themselves. Any time you do this, you repress your awareness of part of your inner life and see that part only as an outer reality. Do it generally enough and you repress your awareness of your entire inner life, and end up a behaviorist. Neurotic? You bet, but very convenient if you don't want to think about your thoughts and feelings.

replies.title: Clicker training
replies.content:
I tried reading Skinner, but he's so boring and filled with academic kitsch I quit.


But Karen Pryor's Clicker Training, in 'Don't Shoot the Dog', is well written (daughter of Philip Wylie, married a Lindberg) and free of kitsch. It has been made to work on everything from dolphins to puppy dogs. And it's pretty simple- watch what animal does, wait till it's as close to what you want as you are likely to get, offer small treat and click your clacker for emphasis. Continue patiently.


So, 'dirt is just matter out of place' and Skinner inspired something useful.

Tried it on small birds myself to no effect, but the clacker is hard for an Orange Seedy Barnacle.

replies.title: Skinner & behaviorists
replies.content:
While the behaviorist project seems self-undermining enough, it's helpful to see it in context. Prior to the formulation of various sorts of radical behaviorism, the (still-forming) field of psychology had some real problems understanding what it was doing, how what it was doing could be justified as actual knowledge, and how its findings could be tested and validated. There was a good deal of what is now sometimes called kafka-trapping going on -- eg, "if you can't see that this claim is true, it just shows how thoroughly your neuroses have distorted your perceptions". (See also Marxist "false consciousness".) Of course, to non-psychologist scientists, the deployment of this kind of argument (in which someone's skepticism invalidated their criticisms) was a prime indicator that the whole enterprise was too confused to take seriously, no matter how plausible some bits of it might seem.

The behaviorist project had two major components: a commitment to materialism, and a commitment to testability. Neither one entails the other -- especially since "materialism" was a fairly poorly defined (and shifting) mix of methodological commitment and metaphysical commitment. (Now, for some people (and I have had conversations with them, so I know they exist), the whole idea of "consciousness" (or "experience") was opaque. It made no sense. It has taken decades to work through all these issues to the point that there is something like a rigorous way of beginning to talk about them. There are people who are exceedingly extraverted, in the Jungian sense. Not that they are the lives of the party, but that they do not spontaneously experience "interiority" as an object of awareness.)

In Skinner's view, introspection existed, but it (a) was not reliable and (b) consisted of aiming the attention at the subset of physiological processes that are available to awareness, which are only a small part of the total set of processes. However, unlike the classical conditioning model, Skinner's model didn't treat organisms as passive objects, but as generators of activities that were then shaped, and reshaped, by their environments by the famous processes of reinforcement (positive and negative) and aversion. How does one find out what counts as reinforcement for a particular organism in a particular situation? One observes, carefully, what the organism does, and what events lead to it doing things more often, or less often. In other words, one first learns from the organism.

The Skinnerian approach is very useful for self-management: it is a systematization of the traditional self-management techniques that go back to the Stoics, if not before. It answers such questions as, "How can I get myself to spend more time studying and less time playing video games?"

It is also very useful for communicating clearly in situations where at least some of the parties haven't a clue as to what communication might be, or what the context is. It is very good for certain kinds of animal training, for example -- but even these applications involve such "hidden variables" as the larger relationship between the trainer and the trainee. An animal, after all, doesn't randomly emit "behaviors". It is trying to get along in a world that it is making sense of as it goes along. Training is a kind of negotiation.

In dealing with people who are desperately confused, or unable to communicate for other reasons, it provides a clear and unambiguous way of setting boundaries, and developing mutuality. In such situations, the hidden variables are even more important, since the methods are effective not through mechanical application, but through laying the foundations (tracks in space) for actual (aha! I'm talking with someone!) insight. As with Socratic dialectic, the person guiding the conversation has to have a real, and humane, understanding of the path to be trodden, and the particular person who is to be treading it -- and has to be open to negotiation, and re-negotiation, as things progress.

Of course, especially when dealing with people, the training model only works when there is something like collaboration.

There is an old, probably apocryphal, story of the psychology class that decided to use positive reinforcement to shape their professor's behavior. They started with nodding and smiling more when he was on one half of the room, and going deadpan when he was on the other. Much to the students' delight, the professor started lecturing from only half the room. So they then tried focusing on moving him to a quarter of the room, which also worked. In the end, they got the poor fellow to give a whole lecture
from the exit door.

In fact, however, people are often pretty good at picking up on manipulation, especially when it's not consistent with what they want. So even a psychology professor might notice that his behavior was being shaped, if it interfered with his larger project of being a psychology professor.

LeGrand

One essential rule for transits, progressions, and all other predictive techniques is that they will not bring you anything that isn't already indicated in your natal chart. If Uranus is conjunct your natal Sun in the 5th -- I don't use vertices so can't speak to that -- and other indications support this, you will tend to be very impulsive and undisciplined in relation to sex, other pleasures, and risk-taking behavior; when Uranus is transiting the 3rd, depending on what else is in your chart, you'll be likely to jump to conclusions about rumors, emails and other communications, especially if they have to do with 5th house matters.

2) It used to be quite common to use the horoscope itself as a memory palace, with each astrological house as a room; the sign of the zodiac on the cusp of each house is on the door of that room, and any planets in that house are indicated by images in that room.

comments.title: A Big Thought Looking for a Small Name and Question on Variation in Pathworking
comments.content:
Good Evening,

To any Americans here, a very happy Independence Day! May you celebrate your freedom in whichever way best suits you.

To Share: On the last Ecosophia Open Post, I asked whether anyone knew about a term for a quality of things/experiences/knowledge that is only (or at least best) recognizable by up-close, personal experience, but no one could point me to exactly what I was looking for, so I decided to try to work it out for myself, and I wrote up my journey so far (long, but hopefully interesting): https://jpowellrussell.com/#a_big_thought_looking_for_a_small_name

To Ask: I'm working my way through pathworking the Wheel of Life, and I've noticed something interesting. Some sessions are pretty much all about the Few, some about the two stations and what links them, some are very conceptual and verbal, and some are very visual and symbolic (like my latest, Straif, which was basically "scrying, as narrated by Carl Gustav Jung"). Is this variety just something to experience, take note of, and meditate on, or are there any rules of thumb to apply (like "go back and pay extra attention wherever it gets vivid and symbolic," or "if it was just a guide talking to you the whole time, you might have missed something," or the like)?

As always, thanks very much to JMG and everyone else here for all that you do.

My blessings to all who welcome them,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: A Big Thought Looking for a Small Name and Question on Variation in Pathworking
replies.content: Just experience it, take note of it, and meditate on it. You can always go back around again and see what happens.

Hi Jeff,

I did the two rounds when i was working with the DMH, first of Scrying the fews, then of pathworking within the framework of the Wheel.

I just wanted to add to JMG response my own advice: Don't hurry in your journey through the wheel, it has a lot to offer but it requires time and focus. I'm still gathering harvest from my own passage, even many months after finishing.

Another advice, although maybe you already know it:While Scrying, you need to adapt to the nature of the region you happen to visit. You'll gain momentum in that way.

May your journey be blessed,

Guillem.

I'm doing my best to follow both pieces of advice. I scried each of the Fews on its own, then scried each of the stations of the Wheel, and only then started in on actual pathworking. For all of these, I spend a minimum of three days meditating on the session afterward, so usually I do one scrying/pathworking a week, 3-4 days meditating on that, and 2-3 days meditating on whatever else I'm currently working on (Dolmen Arch material for the past year or two). With scrying the Fews, I had to go back and scry again a handful where the sessions didn't go so well, and I think that was valuable.

As for adapting to the region visited, that's a bit tougher, but I'm certainly trying to do so. I should go back over my notes and see which regions seemed to go best and what they have in common and maybe get some ideas on what to do in general.

Thank you again!
Jeff

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
One approach to this interesting question is to look at Georges Dumezil's hypothesis concerning the tripartite division of functions within Indo-European societies, and the corresponding functions of Deities in the various Indo-European pantheons. The three functions Dumezil posits are 1) The kings and priests (sacral leadership), 2) the warriors and political leaders (wielders of power in society), and 3) the producers (farmers, crafters, merchants, industry). Dumezil presented Tyr / Odin and Mitra / Varuna as examples of the first-function Deities, Thor and Indra for the 2nd function, and Njordh / Frey and the Asvins for the 3rd function, using the Germanic and Indic Deities as his main examples. These functions can clearly be seen in the Hindu caste divisions that still exist today, subdivided into many further subcategories: the Brahma, Kshatriya, and Vaishya castes.

(Personally, I find this hypothesis limiting and unrealistic when it comes to the deeper natures of specific Deities, but it is a useful construct when studying societies and social functions, and the social roles played by the public rites for various Deities.)

There are, perhaps, several different ways one could compare these three functions with the functions of the Lords of Flame, Form, and Mind; those who are more familiar with the latter concepts than I am might do a better job of this. Here is one set of possibilities:

The Lords of Flame could be compared to the Warrior / power-wielding function, as they deal with the ordering and balancing of powerful planetary and solar-system forces.

The Lords of Form could be seen as the Farmers / Producers, preparing the 'soil' from which life springs.

The Lords of Mind can be compared to the Priestly / sacral Kingship functions as the initiators and guides for the arising of living beings and the natural laws that each type of being must follow.

Getting back to the original question, one could then see specific Deities as being most associated with specific functions which in turn relate to one of the categories of Flame, Form, or Mind, such as the Deity / functions list of Dumezil's that I mentioned above. There has been a good deal of writing published on Dumezil's hypothesis as it relates to various Deities; I'm most familiar with that written about the Germanic / Norse Deities but there is much about other pantheons as well.

As specific examples, Thor as a Warrior / Power-Wielder would be one of the Lords of Flame. Frey as a God of Fertility would be a Lord of Form, while Tyr along with Odin would be among the Lords of Mind. Other Deities could likewise be sorted this way.

Winifred Hodge Rose

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: That's an intriguing concept. I got into a fine quarrel during my short period of membership in ADF by pointing out that the tripartite thing isn't restricted to Indo-European traditions -- the three primary heroes of Coast Salish Native American legend, for example, fall into it very precisely. (Moon the Changer is the sacral figure who teaches everyone the right way to live; Yoo-la-teen is a warrior who kills monsters and climbs to the sky on a bridge of arrows; and Bobcat is the provider, the youngest brother -- of four; that rather than three is the Salish patter number -- who saves the rest and brings home the deer.) So it might be possible to apply it very broadly indeed.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I agree, JMG, that the tripartite social division seems to apply to a great many other societies and mythologies besides the Indo-European, though perhaps there are some exceptions. I find it useful from an anthropological perspective, but limiting and rather forced and unrealistic when applied to the deeper natures of the Deities. I would not agree that any of the Deities I know fit solely into any of these categories. Thor, for example, besides being a warrior and power-wielder, is well-known as a patron of farmers, crafters, and householders, as well as bringing fertility to crops and brides through his Hammer. He is also priest-king of his own mysteries, which are many and deep, including the power to bring his goats back to life after killing and eating them, and being known as Thor-Veurr and Wigi-Thonar: Thor Hallower.

If one went with the idea I wrote earlier, it's interesting that this would make Thor among the oldest Deities as a Lord of Flame; Frey second as a Lord of Form, and Tyr and Odin younger as Lords of Mind. Certainly if one goes with Indo-European language history, Thor's close similarity to the Proto-Indo-European Thunder God *Perkwunos places him as being older than any Deity that can be associated with Odin, as far as I'm aware, though it's awfully difficult to trace such things. And of course the age of Proto-Indo-European language and culture is in no way comparable to the vast ages of the Lords of Flame, Form, and Mind! :-) But presumably humans pick up resonances or traces of such cosmic ages and beings, including their comparative ages, and incorporate them into mythological accounts, though other influences from politics and historical events may later distort them.

Winifred

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
One tantalizing possibility to resolve this particular riddle is the hypothesis that American Indians brought over some of their myths and beliefs from a shared ancestor with the speakers of proto-Indo-European, which I mentioned in response to a question about the World Tree earlier. As I said there, I find it intriguing, but ultimately rather speculative, because it's dealing with such huge swaths of time.

It'd be interesting to compare with groups that lack the proposed shared ancestors to see if it's something different groups arrived at on their own, instead of echoes of something shared very (very) far back.

comments.title: Magic effects on machinery?
comments.content:
I have been beginning to learn the Sphere of Protection, and am still on the first (yellow) gate. This morning when I got to the banishment ("I banish them far from me") I was facing East, and happened to have the yellow gate opened in front of a printer that I assumed was dead--It had not worked in months, and I just had not gotten around to taking it to recycling.
When I pointed thru the gate, the printer came on. Now it works again--

I have never heard that magic could affect mechanical objects. The printer is old (2014-ish bubblejet), so no AI. And I'm not sure why a banishment would restore function to a printer in any case.
Has anyone ever heard/experienced effects of magic on mechanical devices?

replies.title: Re: Magic effects on machinery?
replies.content:
I've seen that sort of thing a few times. I had a coworker, many years ago when I worked in a photocopy shop, who was assigned a long, difficult, repetitive job using a big industrial copier. She hated the job, and every time she would get to the point of being ready to scream, the copier would break down. The repairman couldn't figure out what was going wrong, and when he finally took the whole thing apart he found that toner powder had somehow gotten through an intact seal to gum up the machinery. He insisted that this was physically impossible. The funniest thing about all this is that the coworker was a thoroughgoing atheist and got really freaked out about what was happening...

The challenge to all this is that I've never seen anyone who could do this kind of thing reliably. It just happens now and again!

replies.title: Re: Magic effects on machinery?
replies.content:
I recall from back in the 1980's when I was in computer tech support, I recall one member of the hardware support team - when one of the mainframes was acting up (this is pre-pc), one of the tech guys would stand in front of it, concentrate for a minute and draw a sigil in the air in front of it - and more often than not it started working again. The memory is decades old so I remember no details, but I feel pretty sure about that core of it.

It was a commonplace assumption among us that our state of mind affected the equipment - by far the most common time for abends and crashes was right when a big project was coming due and people were frantic to have the machines working right then... so of course that is when they failed. There is nothing like being pissed off at the computer to get it to fail right then.

I sometimes talk to my printer the way you'd talk to a skittish pet.

replies.title: Re: Magic effects on machinery?
replies.content:
I think a lot of people have observed equipment breaking down at *just* the wrong time.
Obviously, a skeptic would say that this is confirmation bias, or people acting rashly in times of stress, and overlooking something. Still, I've noticed personally that things do seem to break much less frequently when I'm calmly completing a task, regardless of how carefully I'm going about it.
I've also had the fuel light in my car turn off, and the fuel guage move noticeably upwards, offering me a few precious extra miles after praying to Mercury.

Duncan

replies.title: Re: Magic effects on machinery?
replies.content:
More than 40 years ago, back in the days of T-boxes that connected to mainframes via telephone wires, we had a T-box and terminal at home. It was very difficult to get an actual connection: the "working to connect" light would go on and there would be noise, but then soon the attempt would fail and the light would go out -- over and over and over again.

Eventually I developed a magical work-around that usually got a connection on the first try. I would go into a state of mind-and-body where there was nothing in the universe but my will, my sight and the light on the T-box. Once I hit the connect-key, I would gaze intently at the light, holding it "lit" by my will, and never wavering my gaze from it even for an instant. That method almost always got a solid and lasting connection on the very first try. When it didn't, I myself was already fatigued or distracted; so that matters, too.

Yes, everything in the whole universe is sentient, and part of a greater sentience -- even a T-box and telephone link. It can usually be approached on that basis if its own will isn't too opposed to yours. Or at least that's how I commonly experience life ...

replies.title: Re: Magic effects on machinery?
replies.content:
Mechanical things absolutely respond to magic. Even if it is not intentional!

I had a bad day recently. I got the truck jacked up to change the oil, crawled up under, and... didn't have the right wrench to get the filter off. I was pissed and may have uttered some inappropriate words at the underside of the truck. Went inside, started searching the internet for the right tool, every instructional I could pull up was for a different model of truck, I blew my lid, slammed a fist on the desk, said "GAH!"... and my computer shut off. I picked up my phone, right next to me, to call my brother (he's my tech support for all things mechanical) and the phone *had forgotten all my contacts*. Contacts list was completely blank. Both phone and computer worked fine after being restarted (ye olde 'turn it off and turn it back on again' charm). But when I next got in the truck, the electrical outlets (you know, where you plug in the GPS and the phone charger) were not working. Quick fuse check and... sure enough the fuse was blown. I can't say for sure that the fuse blew when I was swearing under the truck, but... it fits with the rest of that day.

Be nice to your machinery!

Incidentally, when people at my church buy a car, they get the priest to bless it... so I'm pretty sure that means my church also endorses the idea that machinery is subject to angelic, demonic, and perhaps other spiritual influences. And, you know, it never hurts to bless the tools you use :)

replies.title: Re: Magic effects on machinery?
replies.content: Two different times I have performed the Dolmen Arch version of healing on the same broken weather radio, and both times it started working again. It's still working.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Good morning JMG,

The Kybalion states that nature tends to develop to the "higher" or "more positive" pole. Considering the plethora of physical examples which are mentioned in the book one can easily identify this as being nonsense, as things usually don't tend to heat up all by themselves but heat flows from hot to cold, and so on. What Atkinson is possibly aiming at is the observation that the living world seems to evolve towards increased complexity. Thinking along these lines, one could argue that on the physical plane the forces of repulsion and attraction (which could be seen as two poles of the same phenomenon in the sense of the Kybalion) are employed against each other to form a thrust block on which (physical) life and complexity can emerge.

So while on the physical plane all forms will eventually decay, the ebb and flow of creation and destruction are used against each other to create, one could say, "abstract moulds" which allow the forms, while still being subject to physical laws, to develop towards more complexity.

Assuming my ponderings are at least broadly correct I still struggle to make the leap to different planes, the astral plane for example. I can imagine the physical plane "imprinting" the astral, for example by creating brains that allow to project both sensory and imaginary input onto the astral - but to imagine in what guise the forces of "attraction and repulsion" can be found on the astral plane (or even beyond), or what general characteristics "attraction" and "repulsion" show on the astral plane still seems to be rather difficult for me.

So, any input is welcome!

Greetings and many thanks,
Nachtgurke

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
This is one of the places where Atkinson let his New Thought background bollix up his occult philosophy. It's a core theme of New Thought that the more you affirm that everything is getting better and better, the more this will happen, and in New Thought jargon words like "better," "positive," and "higher" got muddled together into a soup of incoherent notions. Atkinson's own teachings point to the problem with this: by the Principle of Polarity, there can be no higher without lower, no positive without negative, and no better without worse, and any attempt to move in one of these directions inevitably leads to an equal movement in the other direction.

In traditional occult lore there's a constant twofold movement of undifferentiated forces descending from spirit to matter and differentiated souls ascending from matter to spirit. The living world sometimes moves from simplicity to complexity and at other times moves from complexity to simplicity. Cough, cough, yin and yang, cough, cough...

As for astral attraction and repulsion, though, that's simple enough. The astral plane is the plane of the emotions. Astral attraction is more generally called "love," and astral repulsion "hate." Does that clarify things a bit?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
In the light of New Thought this starts to make sense, yes... What I was trying to say that while creation is subject to ebb and flow, the souls that inhabit creation experience evolution in the CosDoc-sense of the word. Using "law against law" would then amount to using the flow to go forward and then do something to prevent that ebb will take you all the way back. You ride the cycles, so to say. I'm still trying to reconcile this with your valid criticism of Atkinson's logic.

And about the basic forces of the astral plane - yes, that makes sense to me. I wonder, however, if there are more basic ingredients to find on the astral plane. "Love" and "hate" are very human concepts and I am asking myself whether saying "they form the basis of the astral plane" is akin to handing somebody the ten commandments and say "these are the basic principles of the universe" - they are, but only for a cosmos. There are more fundamental laws.

Greetings,
Nachtgurke

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) My take on using law against law is that you balance one thing against something else. For example, you can generate energy to attain one desire by renouncing another desire, or you can lift yourself spiritually by helping something else descend into material manifestation.

2) As I see it, what we call "love" and "hate" are how we experience the cosmic forces of attraction and repulsion. We perceive them in human terms -- being human, we can hardly do anything else -- but they are not themselves human. When somebody in love says "this is bigger than the two of us," that is very often quite precisely correct.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
What about the notion of love more and more every day?
I'm no adept, but I think I've gone in this direction. Am I necessarily hating something else more and more but I'm repressing this? I would think such things might accumulate in my shadow, but I tend to think my shadow is getting just the least bit lighter over time. How would you square these things JMG?

Boston Bob

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I wouldn't. I'd encourage you to meditate on it and come up with your own response.

comments.title: A question about the faculties of the mind
comments.content:
I have noticed in myself that I tend strongly towards synthesis. I think this comes from school, as a math undergrad we are taught to simplify and slowly circle towards a formula by explaining the widest array of phenomena with a single idea.

This process is useful for thought. However, I have noticed that this synthesis-muscle also tends to stifle out the thought process and by having too many of this synthesized seed-thoughts, we become like an echo chamber where the way we perceive and then interpret the world becomes constrained according to out pre-cognitions and become incapable of seeing the world in a new way.

I was wondering, according to this short description, if you would know what would be the opposite to this the tendency to synthesis that would loosen up a bit and allow new perceptions to be interpreted differently.

replies.title: Re: A question about the faculties of the mind
replies.content: The opposite of synthesis is analysis, the process of taking things apart into ever smaller and more diverse elements. Instead of uniting an array of phenomena in a single idea, break the idea apart by seeing how those phenomena differ from each other.

replies.title: Re: A question about the faculties of the mind
replies.content: Sweet. Yes, that makes sense, though the way you put it gives me more to synthesize and analyze about. Thanks!

comments.title: Sat-kona
comments.content:
Dear JMG

I came across this:
*****

I purchased a silver pendant of the “Star of David” from a local shopkeeper and when I put it around my neck I was asked:

“Why are you wearing a Jewish symbol?”

To this I replied:

”This is Sat-kona, the symbol of Goloka, the abode of Krsna.”

This article is about the origins of Sat-kona [the Star of Goloka or Goloka-yantra], its transcendental significance, its historical use in Vedic and other ancient cultures, its use in Christianity and Islam, and its eventual adoption by the Jewish faith in the 17th century as a popular symbol of Judaism.

*****

The page that quote is from shows quite a few examples from around the world:
https://gosai.com/writings/satkona-star-of-david-or-star-of-goloka

"Sat-kona has also enjoyed popular use among alchemists. Alchemists of old and particularly those from the 5th century to the 15th century in Europe and the Middle East were very consistent in their use of Sat-kona. For most cultures the Sat-kona symbolized male [the upward triangle] and female [the downward triangle] or just something auspicious, but for the Alchemist the Sat-kona symbolized fire and water. Alchemists also used the Sat-kona to represent the six planets [each of the 6 points] and the sun was in the middle of the hexagram. The symbol universally represented the art of Alchemy for Muslims, Christians and Jews as the representation of the combination of opposites and transmutation."

Edit:
JMG Jan 2024: "No, the use of the star tetrahedron as an emblem for much of anything was invented by Bernard Perona aka Drunvalo Melchizedek in the late 20th century."

The dual of the tetrahedron is shown as nesting within, but since the Platonic solids appear to be snapshots of processes in flow, the idea of them overlapping as a 3D hexagram does not seem unreasonable?
Do you have any thoughts on his work/ideas?

Thank you.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hello JMG and the Magic Monday community.

I started my occult path by going towards alchemy (based on the works of Dubuis and Bardon). However, I am puzzled by the lack of physical exercises (apart from the residual elements in Bardon).

I came across your book some time ago, Mr Greer on the 5 Tibetan rituals, I see that they work with a previously unknown to me system of 7 wortexes. Fascinating work.

Regarding my questions:
Can the 5 Tibetan rituals be combined with the theory of the 3 dantiems present in Taoist alchemy (and also partly in the European energy system)
How do you find Tai chi and Qi gong as a physical addition to the adept's alchemical development? I have sometimes come across theories that Tai chi and Qi gong are, because of their origins, too distant for the European soul and should not be practised for this reason, but personally I would not lean in this direction.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
The physical exercises of the Western esoteric tradition were among the things that got lost in the two great crises of the tradition -- the triumph of intolerant Christianity in the 6th century CE and the triumph of intolerant materialism in the 17th century CE. We're still picking up a lot of the pieces that were dropped back then.

1) No, it has its own system of energy centers. It's important to respect the specific energy centers used by any given system of practices, because different systems using different centers don't always play well together.

2) I had to drop taijiquan and qigong entirely more than a decade ago because practicing them along with my Western esoteric practices gave me a case of kidney yang qi deficiency -- a condition that could have wrecked my health if I hadn't taken immediate action. (As it was, it took some years for me to get over it and return to relatively good health.) That's the sort of problem that can happen when you combine different systems of energy work!

I have found that several other systems of exercise are entirely compatible with the Western esoteric work I do; the Five Rites work well in this context, so do the exercises William Walker Atkinson (under the pen name "Yogi Ramacharaka") included in his book Hatha Yoga; so do ordinary hatha yoga exercises, though I'm not at all sure about the more demanding end of pranayama or the various bandhas; so do Japanese martial arts -- aikido and shintaido in particular play well with Western occultism -- and so does an odd offshoot of taijiquan, Tai Chi Chih, which was invented by an American taiji teacher named Justin Stone. But I know of other Western occultists who have had bad results from combining their work with qigong, so I can't recommend that at all.

replies.title: Re: trees, part III
replies.content:
1) Since I wasn't there, how can I confirm it? In my experience, different regions have their own personalities, which affect living things in those regions. Whether that's what's going on here, though, only those who are there can judge.

replies.title: Re: trees, part III
replies.content:
On 2), as JMG says, it's a very big topic, and my own familiarity is mostly with the Germanic flavor of things, which it sounds like you're at least somewhat familiar with, but a few places you might look more into it, if you like:

a. If you do videos, Tom Rowsell of Survive the Jive talks a fair amount about the World Tree in "The World's Oldest Myth?": https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=exlcT_iL9-U

The short version is that you have some intriguing traces of common elements (a tree that is the world, with roots in the land of the dead, that are tended by female divine figures who are very wise, and guarded by dogs, among a few others) in Indo-European-derived religions, the circumpolar shamanic beliefs, and in the myths and practices of some American Indians. Rowsell presents it as a slam dunk that this means such beliefs go back to the shared ancestors of the Proto-Indo-European speakers, circumpolar peoples, and the American Indians, whereas I think it's quite suggestive, but not definitive.

b. Speaking of circumpolar shamans, Mircea Eliade's Shamanism: Archaic Techniques of Ecstasy includes discussion of the role trees and the world tree play in their beliefs. I believe he also touches on the World Tree in the History of Religious Ideas, but not sure which volume, as I just got my copies and haven't been able to dig in yet.

c. Mostly on the Scandinavian side of things, but in The Seed of Yggdrasill, Maria Kvilhaug talks about a lot of different topics in Norse/Germanic mythology, but as the title suggests, the symbolic role of the World Tree is fairly central to much of it. She seems to most strongly favor the idea of the World Tree as a symbol for the subtle body of man parallel to Indian beliefs and practices, with the dragon Nidhogg at the roots representing the kundalini, and the eagle and hawk at the top representing the third eye and crown chakras. Certainly interesting, but it strikes me as pretty speculative.

Cheers, and hope this helps,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: That’s a problem for me
replies.content:
The gods are not your mommy. Nor are they vending machines that will cough up goodies, or affection, or anything else if you pop your prayer coin in the slot. They are the personified primary powers of the cosmos. You can establish a relationship with them, and they'll honor that, but only to the extent that you honor it -- and them. They have a lot to do, and the interests of one little incarnate being do not weigh that heavily in the great scheme of their aeonian lives.

Compare it to dealing with people. Some random person off the street has no reason to care about you, much less give you what you want just because you ask for it. If you befriend that person, spend time with them, and help them when they ask, then it's a different matter. The same thing is true of gods and goddesses -- except, of course, that they're considerably wiser and stronger than you and I, they know what we're thinking and feeling, and they've seen every possible combination of human antics more times than we can count. They've seen it all. That means you can't bamboozle them, or lie to them, or pressure them -- but you can turn to them honestly in prayer.

replies.title: Re: That’s a problem for me
replies.content:
I agree with what JMG says here, but want to note that compassion can definitely be found among the Deities (and also note that while some Gods are not our mommy, there are Goddesses who specifically *are* our mommy, though on an awe-inspiring and non-indulgent level!) Jesus and Mother Mary and Kwan Yin are not the only ones who have compassion to offer. But we need to understand clearly what compassion is, and realize that different Deities, as well as people, offer compassion in different forms. Sometimes those forms, like genuine 'tough love', are not the kind of compassion we thought we wanted, but are the kind of compassion we honestly need. The section "Thor's Act of Compassion" in my article "Thoughts on Thor and his Children" gives one example of that. (HeathenSoulLore.net)

Compassion, as well as being an act or an attitude, is a form of energy, and like all other forms of energy it flows to and from and through the Deities and through us. I've written about this mystery of compassion and given examples of different kinds of compassion offered by different Heathen Deities in my article "Experience and Practice of Compassion in Heathenry." I don't think what I've written is specific to Heathenry, though; it can apply in other contexts, religious and non-religious, as well. Different Deities offer different 'flavors' and 'strengths' of compassion, which may disappoint us when we don't find the 'flavor' we're looking for from a given Deity, and it's certainly true that some are stronger channels of compassion toward us than others are.

I might note that compassion--which simply means 'feeling together, mutual experience'--is something one is more likely to experience through mystical, as opposed to magical or occult, approaches to and relationships with the Deities. The path of faithful friendship with the Deities, as JMG notes, is the entry to productive work and relationships with them in magical, occult, and mystical contexts alike.

A note on Odin's compassion...in spite of being a difficult and daunting Deity, modern Heathens feel that he is a primary mover and shaker in influencing people toward Heathenry, and that his title of Allfather refers more to his relationship to humankind as a patron Deity than to any generative role he might play. His compassion is more general and impersonal, more focused on larger groups of humans than on one individual at a time. But in spite of all his twistiness there is goodwill there: instead of indulging us in our selfish desires, he draws our attention to the bigger picture, the longer view, the deeper mysteries.

In the past and the present, people have often complained that Odin's chosen have come to early and bad ends. As a response to this complaint, I love this verse from J.R.R. Tolkien's epic poem "The Legend of Sigurd and Gudrun." It is worthy of good discursive meditation!

Whom Odin chooseth endeth not untimely,
Though ways of men he walk briefly.
In wide Valholl he may wait feasting:
It is to ages after that Odin looks.
(Verse 14, p. 70-1)

Winifred Hodge Rose

replies.title: Re: relating with the gods
replies.content: Remember that Greek religious poetry used epithets to indicate what they wanted gods to do. The Furies were called the Eumenides, "the soothed ones," precisely because they were so hard to placate; those who made offerings to them used that title for magical reasons. In the same way, Hermes is a tricky god -- originally the god of the old straight track and the herms (standing stones or rock cairns) that marked its passage, and thus of the subtle and dangerous force that flows along the track -- and so the Orphics, who were no fools (and occult initiates to boot), called him by euphemisms like these to encourage him to be friendly and helpful.

I'm kind of on the other side of the partition from you: I've never done any drugs, have always been able to feel out the energy of places and people... but I also have a lot of weird sensory artefacts that I have only ever found descriptions of in the psychedelic literature-- like HPPD but without the drugs. 4) Did you pray or meditate at all before this happened? Have you tried it since? And if so has it affected your practice? (that is not a recommendation btw: some of the literature on HPPD suggests that if you experience dissociation after a trip, meditation is not recommended!)

Anyway, answers or no, I wish you all the best, and is it OK if I pray for you? I feel awful for you reading your post, as that sense is very important to me and I'd feel amputated without it :/

One kinda-sorta exception to JMG's answer that may or may not be what you have in mind: in the 20th century Armanen Rune Revival tradition, part of Ariosophy (which got a bad name for being associated with German nationalism), there was a set of hand gestures for each Rune. I've not really done any work with them, and so I can't speak to what kind of esoteric work they do or don't do, or how similar they are to Asian mudras, but they are hand gestures with esoteric intent from a Western tradition (very very likely influenced by early exposure in the West to Asian mudras, but hey).

This occurred to me, too, but I never got around to developing a system for using them. (Caroline Jayne's and Kathleen Haddon's books on many various Cat's Cradles are long out of copyright, and can be downloaded from various sites.) Like methods of complex knotting, weaving and spiunning, their recurring patterns seem to me very well suited for spell-casting, and also for scrying.

Sir Walter Scott touches on this in one of his poems (from "Guy Mannering"):

Twist ye, twine ye! even so,
Mingle shades of joy and woe,
Hope, and fear, and peace, and strife,
In the thread of human life.

While the mystic twist is spinning,
And the infant's life beginning,
Dimly seen through twilingt bending,
Lo, what varied shapes attending!

Passions wild, and follies vain,
Pleasures soon exchanged for pain;
Dount, and jealousy, and fear,
In the magic dance appear.

Now they wax, and now they dwindle,
Whirling with the whirling spindle.
Twist ye, twine ye! even so,
Mingle human bliss and woe.

comments.title: Earth's Epochs and Cosmogenesis
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

Are you familiar with esoteric writer, astrologer and cosmologist Phillip Lindsay (author of "Unveiling Genesis" et al) and the cosmogensesis put forth in this book and his teaching? Do you see any empirical evidence for the existence of Atlantis and Lemuria, or at least enough anomalous, anachronistic, or unexplained/unexplainable archeological discoveries to cast in doubt the official narrative that the earth is just 4.5 billion years old (although maybe that's describing merely our current earth's current globe-round or chain??) and the universe a mere young pup at 13 billion years? Prominent examples you would point others to as "negative evidence" against the official narrative?

Thank you as always!

replies.title: Re: Earth's Epochs and Cosmogenesis
replies.content: I'm not familiar with Lindsay. I do think there is evidence for high civilizations during the last ice age, though that was recent enough that it doesn't cast doubt on the currently accepted age of the earth -- Plato, remember, who is the original source for the Atlantis account, dates its sinking to 9600 BCE, right when the last ice age ended, and I have my doubts about those occult teachers who want to push its dates far into the past. Myself, it seems quite reasonable that the earth in its current incarnation is just a few billion years old -- a billion years is an insanely long time! -- but the notion that the universe exploded out of nothing at any point in the past strikes me as an odd one at best, the kind of thing that will likely be laughed at by future researchers. I'll see if I can find a copy of Lindsay's book at some point, when time permits, and give it a look.

replies.title: Re: Earth's Epochs and Cosmogenesis
replies.content:
If I recall correctly, Lindsay believes that Atlantis is an "umbrella" term for a large civilization/epoch that underwent 4 or 5 different floods, the final and most recent/famous of which (9600 BCE) was actually the flood recounted in the Old Testament story of Noah (who became the manu or Lord of the new civilization) and notably alluded to by Plato.

My follow-up is, What then is your best guess/hypothesis on the origin of the universe/creation? If it didn't start from a single speck/point, is it eternal, no beginning or end? (e.g. in the Mahatma Letters we read that matter and energy are eternal and two sides of same coin, and even if matter didn't exist there would be energy churning through dark space eternally). Does it require a "First/Prime Cause" (i.e. God or a god) to commence? Is this whole question unanswerable to the structure of finite minds?

Thank you!

replies.title: Re: Earth's Epochs and Cosmogenesis
replies.content:
The entire question of whether the universe had a beginning or not is an absurdity. Immanuel Kant showed with perfect clarity more than two hundred years ago that time is a condition of human consciousness, not an objective reality -- a realization that mystics everywhere have been pointing out for millennia. Claiming that the universe either had or did not have a beginning, on the other hand, only makes sense if time is an objective reality. So the whole issue's one of misplaced concreteness -- it's like trying to measure in yards the distance you walked in your dreams.

As far as Atlantis is concerned, I see it as a label for a cycle of civilizations thousands of years long, and covering a very large share of the habitable world of the ice age. The Lemurian, Hyperborean, and Polarian epochs before it were likewise whole cycles of civilizations, the first two in the last interglacial, the third in the early ice age. That said, all four of them took place well within the last million years -- 1/600th of the period that there have been multicellular life forms. It's quite possible that there were other intelligent species on this planet long before then, and that there will be others long after we're gone.
